sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “Go, Swift Messengers, to a People Tall and Smooth-Skinned”

heb.paragraph: "Woe to the land of whirring (ṣirṣar) wings along the rivers of Cush" (v.1). Messengers sent in papyrus boats on the waters (v.2). The description: a people tall and smooth-skinned, feared far and wide, a nation aggressive and of strange speech (v.2). "All you people of the world, when a banner is raised on the mountains, you will see it; when a trumpet sounds, you will hear it" (v.3).

panels.ctx: Cush (Ethiopia/Nubia, modern Sudan) was a significant power in Isaiah’s time — the 25th Egyptian Dynasty was Cushite (c.747–656 BC). The "whirring wings" may refer to the tsetse fly or the sound of many papyrus boats. The Cushites were seeking an anti-Assyrian alliance with Judah. Isaiah’s oracle does not condemn Cush but redirects: wait for God’s action, not political alliances. The "banner on the mountains" (v.3) is God’s signal to the whole world.

cross.note: An Ethiopian eunuch — the first African convert, connecting Cush to the church.
Cush will submit herself to God — the psalm anticipating Cush’s eventual worship.
From beyond the rivers of Cush my worshippers will bring me offerings — the same geographic hope.
The products of Cush will come over to you and will be yours — Cush’s eventual submission to God’s people.

notes.note: Cush sends envoys in papyrus boats seeking alliance against Assyria. Isaiah redirects: God will act, not alliances.
A banner raised, a trumpet sounded: when God acts, the whole world will see. No embassy needed.
Calvin reads the oracle as a warning against political alliances that bypass God: Cush offers military help, but God’s plan requires trust, not treaties.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET identifies the papyrus boats as the lightweight craft used on the Upper Nile, made from papyrus reeds bundled together. The same vessel-type appears in Egyptian art.
The NET notes "tall and smooth-skinned" reflects the physical appearance of Nilotic peoples, described with admiration rather than prejudice.
Oswalt reads the Cush oracle as part of Isaiah’s consistent anti-alliance theology: trust God, not Egypt/Cush. The pattern recurs in chs.20, 30–31.
Oswalt: the banner-and-trumpet verse universalises the message: God’s action is public and global, not private and local.
Childs reads the oracle as canonical witness to God’s sovereignty over Africa: Cush is neither enemy nor saviour but a nation under God’s governance like all others.
Childs notes the universal address ("all you people of the world") breaks out of the Cush-specific context into eschatological scope.

sections.header: Verses 4–7 — “Gifts Will Be Brought to the LORD Almighty”

heb.paragraph: "At that time gifts (minḥâ) will be brought to the LORD Almighty from a people tall and smooth-skinned, feared far and wide — to Mount Zion, the place of the Name of the LORD Almighty" (v.7). God watches quietly, like shimmering heat and a cloud of dew (v.4). Before the harvest, he cuts the shoots and removes the branches (v.5). The pruned branches are left for the birds and animals (v.6). Then: Cush brings gifts to Zion (v.7).

panels.ctx: The oracle ends not with Cush’s destruction but with Cush’s worship — gifts brought to Zion. This is remarkable: the nation that sought a military alliance is redirected to a worship-relationship. The pruning metaphor (vv.5–6) suggests God allows Assyria’s plans to develop but cuts them before completion. Verse 7 anticipates the universal worship of 2:2–4 and 60:1–7. The Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8 is often read as a partial fulfilment.

cross.note: Nations bring gold and incense to Zion — the expanded version of 18:7’s gift-bringing.
I will record Rahab and Babylon among those who acknowledge me, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush — Cush listed among God’s worshippers.
The Ethiopian eunuch converts — the first African believer.
The Magi offered gifts of gold, frankincense, myrrh — nations bringing gifts to Zion’s king.

notes.note: God watches like shimmering heat. He waits. Then before harvest he prunes. The timing is God’s, not ours.
Cush brings gifts to Zion. The oracle ends in worship, not destruction. The enemy becomes a worshipper.
Calvin reads the quiet-watching metaphor as God’s patience: he does not act immediately but waits for the right moment. The silence is not absence but strategic restraint.
Calvin: Cush bringing gifts to Zion is the missionary promise embedded in a judgment oracle. Every nation’s destiny is either destruction or worship. God offers worship.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "shimmering heat" and "dew-cloud" describe God’s presence as pervasive but invisible. He is there, watching, but not yet intervening.
The NET observes "the place of the Name" is a Deuteronomic formula for the temple (Deut 12:5, 11). Cush’s gifts go to the temple, not to a political ally.
Oswalt reads the end-in-worship as the Cush oracle’s distinctive contribution: not all nations-oracles end in doom. Some end in conversion. Cush is among the latter.
Oswalt: gifts to Mount Zion from the ends of the earth anticipates the Gentile mission. Isaiah’s vision is consistently universal.

Childs reads the oracle as a canonical testimony to God’s patient sovereignty: he watches quietly (v.4), prunes at the right time (v.5), and receives worship from the nations (v.7). The sequence is universal.
Childs notes the gift-bringing connects to 2:2–4 (nations stream to Zion) and forward to 60:1–7 (nations bring their wealth). The canonical trajectory is toward universal worship.

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: The Cushites were seeking an anti-Assyrian alliance with Judah…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThey will all be left to the mountain birds of prey and to the wild animals; the birds will feed on them all summer, the wild animals all winter.ESVThey shall all of them be left to the birds of prey of the mountains and to the beasts of the earth. And the birds of prey will summer on them, and all the beasts of the earth will winter on them.NIVAt that time gifts will be brought to the Lord Almighty from a people tall and smooth-skinned, from a people feared far and wide, an aggressive nation of strange speech, whose land is divided by rivers\u2014 the gifts will be brought to Mount Zion, the place of the Name of the Lord Almighty.ESVAt that time tribute will be brought to the LORD of hosts from a people tall and smooth, from a people feared near and far, a nation mighty and conquering, whose land the rivers divide, to Mount Zion, the place of the name of the LORD of hosts.

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: A clay cylinder in which Cyrus of Persia describes his conquest of Babylon and his policy of returning captive peoples to their homelands — the Persian policy that fulfilled Isaiah 44:28–45:1.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: Sennacherib’s own account of his 701 BC campaign states he shut Hezekiah up “like a bird in a cage” in Jerusalem — but conspicuously does NOT claim to have captured the city.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "Cush sends envoys in papyrus boats seeking alliance against Assyria. Isaiah redirects: God will act, not alliances."

rows.label: Swift Messengers — 18:1–3
rows.text: Cush sends envoys seeking alliance. "ALL you people of the world — when a BANNER is raised, you will see it."

hebtext.note: "Woe to the land of whirring (ṣirṣar) wings along the rivers of Cush" (v.1). Messengers sent in papyrus boats on the waters (v.2). The description: a people tall and smooth-skinned, feared far and wide, a nation aggressive and of strange speech (v.2). "All you people of the world, when a banner is raised on the mountains, you will see it; when a trumpet sounds, you will hear it" (v.3).

"At that time gifts (minḥâ) will be brought to the LORD Almighty from a people tall and smooth-skinned, feared far and wide — to Mount Zion, the place of the Name of the LORD Almighty" (v.7). God watches quietly, like shimmering heat and a cloud of dew (v.4). Before the harvest, he cuts the shoots and removes the branches (v.5). The pruned branches are left for the birds and animals (v.6). Then: Cush brings gifts to Zion (v.7).

Cush will submit herself to God — the psalm anticipating Cush’s eventual worship.
From beyond the rivers of Cush my worshippers will bring me offerings — the same geographic hope.
The products of Cush will come over to you and will be yours — Cush’s eventual submission to God’s people.
Nations bring gold and incense to Zion — the expanded version of 18:7’s gift-bringing.
I will record Rahab and Babylon among those who acknowledge me, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush — Cush listed among God’s worshippers.
